replies.author: @Impurity41
replies.text:
I played it. Gave it a fair shot. Even solo’d some squads. I’ve played destiny as a series for a really long time but quit some time ago. 

I wrote up a longer review in my notes but the “spark notes” are: 

Gunplay is…fine. I don’t like the ttk, personally I think it’s too fast. The consumables are all over the place. Your basic heals are shit. I get basic healing is supposed to be crap but take the worst healing in arc raiders and make it heal like 30-40% less and that’s marathon’s on a faster ttk. Omg. 

The heat system is asscheeks, it has different character abilities making it a “hero shooter” for an extraction game which is odd, and it has one of the worst UIs and new player experiences for a game that I’ve seen in awhile.

Every single piece of loot in the game, a game where you need to loot fast and can get shot at any second, has multiple full paragraphs of lore on a single item and takes up a large part of the screen. 

I get “abstract” was the theme here, but it shouldn’t be that way for the UI. Story and some items can be abstract. Systems and UI should not be. Function before form. If it’s not good, no one will care.

The buildings looked like they were colored in a way that looked like a dev test build to show what buildings you want colored a certain way once you get to the next phase of the build. The fact that the buildings are actually green on one side, black on the other, and pink or red for the rest is just…not pleasing to look at. 

People that have prepared models for color mapping will know what I mean. Like if you label one side of a building as “1”, then the second side is “2”, and the rest of the sides are labeled “3”, then you map the colors and textures. Right before you put the colors and textures on is what these buildings look like to me in their current state.

People are just glazing products without even beginning to understand how others might feel about it. They need to be able to look at a game and understand what works and doesn’t work. And you should be able to see it whether someone likes the product or not. 

There are plenty of games I’ve liked that I’ve gone “most people probably won’t like this for x reasons” and plenty that I don’t like where I can say “I can see why people like this even though I don’t”.

These people, DONT have that. They just think it’s hate.

replies.author: @Doomweapon66
replies.text:
@joshthesquash9259 As an ex-Destiny player who's played it from the D1 Beta all the way to the end of Final Shape, I look at "new Marathon" as having the following set of issues from what I've seen (this'll be a long read so be ready):
1. The gunplay is reskinned D2 gunplay. Now, I'll be fair and say that even though I really don't like D2, I will say the gunplay is one of the few things from it that I think works particularly well even now. The catch is I argue a good chunk of D2's most particular standout points of it's gunplay comes from exotics and the weapon perks however. The problem with regards to Marathon reusing that gunplay is I'm guessing it's more similar to "D2 launch" type of weapons than what D2's guns are like now (set perks, set scopes, etc.). This means you're missing 2 of the crucial chunks that I argue makes D2 gunplay particularly good and the remaining chunks are whether you're using the guns in their intended ranges and the guns themselves. 
If you don't do something dumb like what Smash JT did in his clip of playing "new Marathon" (I'll throw shade at him for this but he may just not have experience with D2's gunplay so I can "give him a pass" to some extent) where he appeared to be using a 900 RPM SMG to tap fire a turret or something at range, then chances are the guns will feel a bit better. But that's where I will go to point 2 now.

2. My guess is "new Marathon" will suffer the same fate D2 did with it's balancing issues, which are most notably illustrated through the infamous tale of the Dead Man's Tale in D2. 
In case you never played D2, Dead Man's was the first of the 120 scout rifles and it's entire thing was it was basically a cowboy repeater. Hip firing with it gave you more fire rate, more accuracy, and more headshot damage. Dead Man's, when it first launched, was able to 2 shot headshot in PvP modes with basically minimal effort to do so. It was pretty OP tbh and the subsequent nerf made sense but also shows the problem with "new Bungie's" balancing strategy.
The nerf ended up reducing the damage on Dead Man's to make it a 3 shot headshot weapon instead of a 2 shot. The problem with this was that 2 shot headshots was infinitely too fast but 3 shots was too slow and would usually result in you just getting destroyed if the person you were fighting was using an AR, SMG, or something else that has a fast TTK and they have solid enough accuracy. This eventually went back and forth and basically never truly got resolved because it came down to the weapon just being too slow for 3 shots but 2 shot headshots was basically complete BS too. The last I ever remember of what happened to it was they basically reduced the hip fire accuracy and that *somewhat* "helped" but it was still stuck in that limbo state.
I should also note that this isn't a new thing either. "New Bungie" has a bad tendency of trying to constantly nerf the metas within D2 and it's an extremely vicious cycle that has been around since forever. And it almost always ends up the same way PvP (and even PvE wise to some extent) of people going and finding a new meta to spam instead. 
Why? Because D2's PvP isn't treated the same way as the old Halo-era Bungie PvP was. Gone are the days of the old Bungie-Halo casual "party game" style PvP where competitive was secondary and fun was the priority. D2 and seemingly "new Marathon" both fell into the exact same trap as the vast majority of PvP of nowadays, where they are meant to be competitive first and foremost. And while there isn't anything technically wrong with that, I would argue it's also therefore to be expected that people will use whatever is the most powerful gun that feels good to use (i.e. I used to play Titanfall 2 PvP a lot and the Spitfire has always reigned supreme there because it's got a good RoF, good damage, lots of ammo, and high accuracy).

3. "New Bungie" has always had a problem with how they handle player feedback. It's even something that became a huge meme with even some of the people who love D2 that any time you try to bring a good idea or a desire to see something changed, "new Bungie" always reacts with "we're listening" and that's sometimes where it stops. Hell, my understanding from what I tangentially heard through a couple of my friends who also tangentially keep up with D2 is a lot of the things people had been asking for over the years only got added after Final Shape was done, even though "new Bungie" also said a few of those features were "too difficult to do" (IIRC they did something to grenade cooldowns after Final Shape but I don't remember for sure. I just stopped caring for D2 in general after a while).

4. The color design, enemy design, player character design, and the environment design are my biggest annoyance and it's because I've seen it done better by "old Bungie" during the days of Halo, which especially annoys me since "new Bungie's" devs try to claim they made Halo (should note that there's basically nobody from the Halo days, or at least none of the people who made Halo Halo like Marty or Jason Jones or Jaime Griesemer, still at "new Bungie." Lot of them left after Reach, lot of them left over the course of D2, Marty got forced out after Forsaken, and Michael Salvatori, the other composer form the Halo days, got laid off just before Final Shape).
Here's my primary example: The elites in Halo 1 and 2. Each rank has an armor color that corresponds with their rank. It goes blue, red, white (ultras didn't exist in Halo 1 notably enough), stealth elites use pale silver in Halo 1 and brownish colored armor in Halo 2, gold, and then spec ops elites use all black in Halo 1 and then a combination of black or a dark blackish purple (almost the same color as a bruise) in Halo 2. They are color coded strictly by these ranks, which is important because in Halo 1 and 2, they all used the exact same armor.
This is important to note because it means all the elites are very easily identifiable by their armor. And said armor is using bright enough colors that you can usually tell what elite rank you're looking at by a glance from even sniper rifle ranges (basically, as long as their model is loaded in and visible without a sniper scope or a zoom in, then you can immediately identify their rank and where they are). 
This is especially important because the elites' armor makes them stand out against the environments 95% of the time. The missions Delta Halo and Regret in Halo 2 are great illustrations of this because a red or blue elite will always stand out against the "stereotypical ancient ruins tanned brown" color of the structures in Regret (this is assuming you're not dealing with High Charity levels of really dark environments where you sometimes just enough to know there's an enemy but not be able to see much beyond that. That said, High Charity is generous because the only enemies there unmodded are brutes, jackals, and flood and all are pretty identifiable even under bad lighting).
So, here's the problem when you consider that in mind: You probably have fought bosses or enemies before in something like Dead Space or God knows how many other games where the enemy has some bright yellow glowing "shoot me here" type of organ or whatever that lets you do damage or do more damage to the enemy. Bright colors like that tend to drag the eye towards them more than anything else. That's been something games have done since forever. 
"New Marathon" theoretically turns this on it's head in the manmade structures where the environment on the outdoors is bright colors and the players/enemies are "dull colors" like white, grey, or black. I say "theoretically" because this gets thrown out the window partially when indoors, where the environments indoors can sometimes be bright colors but also can be grey, white, black, or some other darker shade of an existing color. 
The end result is that the standard "prioritizing targets" that people tend to do gets thrown off balance from this, especially since some of the AI are in all black armor just like one of the player characters. And that means there's possible scenarios where you see someone walking in the distance and you're forced to use some form of zoom in to figure out if that black armored figure is a bot or a player that's just botwalking like an idiot.

That's the reasons I could come up with on actual criticisms for the game and even though you can argue some of it is speculative, I would argue I've got enough experience with D2 and "new Bungie" and the fact that it's a lot of the same people who worked on D2 PvP working on "new Marathon" PvP that gives me a good enough set of reasons to be "wary" at best. Maybe this'll turn out differently than I expect (technically the game not immediately falling to Highguard player counts already changed the "equation" a little bit but I don't think the ultimate outcome changed. I just think it extended the timeframe). I just don't have any default reason nor have I been given enough of a reason to think "this'll be different."

replies.author: @Joonixx
replies.text: @SchemingGoldberg Can we PLEASE for the love of god stop beating the (at this point) rotten molding dead corpse of the word "hate"? Not EVERY criticism is hate. In fact I'd argue that at least 90% of criticisms of any video game is legitimate (whether or not you personally agree with their criticisms). People just love to over simplify every negative comment as "hate" and it's genuinely brain rot. Hating would be shitting on a game not because you have issues with it or dislike X or Y thing about it but rather you just hate that genre or dislike the devs/publisher and want their game to fail because you hate seeing your favorite streamer/content creators all streaming it. Saying a game just doesn't feel like it is compelling enough to play long term (which is what a live service game needs to be) is a valid criticism. Hating on it would be someone saying they hate bungie for ditching Halo or Destiny for this game and they hope it fails so Bungie learns their lesson. That is hate and not valid criticism because it doesn't argue a point with merit or critique of an aspect or mechanic or anything like that.

replies.author: @Xatagarasu
replies.text:
@s0urcl0@s0urcl0ut played it and are you the target audience? There is so much nuance and depth to the gameplay it’s frustrating because all I’m seeing is the same; ui is trash, game looks bad, battle royale complaints. No one talks about the games unique use of weather to influence your stamina resources. The game has some of the most selfish yet team play benefitting systems. Healer drone attached to two different targets lets you share healing items, smoke ults are much easier to be used for the benefit of your team rather than your individual use case. Pinging enemies gives you brief moments of wall hacks that make team communication much easier, instead of a ping showing a potential enemy, it actively tracks the enemy player for 2 seconds. These are innovations, they aren’t necessarily identifiable after your first game but you really come to appreciate them after you sink 20 hours in. 

Raid mechanic puzzles, crisp gunplay, and sound design, are what imo is the X factor for marathon. We have an arg going on right now to discover and unlock cryo archive. Community wide event. With cryo archive boasting raid puzzles I think that will be very new to the extraction shooter formula. 

The PvP in this game is much better than any other extraction I’ve played. Namely arc raiders. I’ve been able to 1vX many times as an average PvPer due to combining slides, mind games, and aim. It’s really refreshing to actually feel like my actions made a difference in the gunfight whereas in a lot of these extraction shooters it’s whoever shot first gets the win.

The icons are definitely less descriptive than they should be but the menu hate feels super forced. 

Feels to me that no one looked at the tutorial and just skipped it asap then got confused because something glaringly obvious was ignored. I’ve got literal schizo friends who understood the ui because they at least read the instructions. I could go on but it’s pointless. People are gonna hate and there’s no point to trying to change the opinion of someone who didn’t even come up with their own.
